channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: House Votes to Extend Expiring Law on Warrantless Surveillance for 10 Days
item.description: The Senate would need to also approve the stopgap measure that passed the House early Friday. Libertarian-leaning House Republicans had balked at a long-term extension.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage
item.category: Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Gen. Joshua M. Rudd, the director of the National Security Agency, during his confirmation hearing in January.

item.title: Trump Promotes ‘No Tax on Tips’ in Las Vegas and Brushes Off Iran War Inflation Concerns
item.description: At an economic event, the president sought to downplay the financial hardship that has followed his war with Iran, saying that “we’re having some fake inflation because of the fuel, the energy prices.”
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump sought to give an affordability message in speaking Thursday about his tax policies to workers in Las Vegas.

item.title: Acting ICE Director Says He Plans to Resign in May
item.description: Todd Lyons said he would leave to spend more time with his family. He has spoken about a surge in threats against ICE officers, saying that he knew the reality firsthand.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The exit of Todd Lyons as acting director of Immigration and Customs Enforcement raises more questions about the direction of the agency, which has faced dimming public opinion.

item.title: Former Navy Service Member Charged in Georgia Killing Spree
item.description: The suspect is accused of carrying out a string of attacks in the Atlanta area that killed two women and injured a homeless man. The authorities described the attacks as random.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Ben Hendren/Atlanta Journal-Constitution, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The street where Lauren Bullis, a Department of Homeland Security employee, was killed while walking her dog on Monday in Decatur, Ga.

item.title: F.A.A. Caps Flights at O’Hare Airport in Chicago to Cut Delays
item.description: Set to be in place throughout the summer, the limit aims to ease congestion after less than 60 percent of flights ran on schedule.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Air traffic around O’Hare International Airport has surged in recent years.

item.title: Officials Investigate Bomb Threat at Home of Pope’s Brother
item.description: The police in suburban Chicago said they found no explosive devices. The investigation comes amid a public dispute between the pope and President Trump over the Iran war.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Patrick Meinhardt/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The investigation follows days of tension between the pope and President Trump over the Iran war.

item.title: Michigan’s Dam Crisis Shows Decay of U.S. Infrastructure, Whitmer Says
item.description: Workers in Cheboygan hurried to shore up a dam in danger of being overtopped, part of a “slow-moving disaster” threatening communities across the state.
item.dc:creator: Billy Witz, Sam Easter and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Jan-Michael Stump/Traverse City Record-Eagle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A team of municipality workers cleared debris from the Boardman River Weir that was blocking the flow of the flooded Boardman/Ottaway River in downtown Traverse City, Mich., on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump to Pick Ousted FEMA Head to Lead Agency Again
item.description: Cameron Hamilton is a former Navy SEAL who ran unsuccessfully for Congress.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Scott Dance and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:credit: Jose Luis Magana/Associated Press
item.media:description: Cameron Hamilton told a House committee last May, when he was the acting chief of FEMA, that the agency was vital to communities “in their greatest times of need” and should not be eliminated. He was ousted the next day.

item.title: How Gavin Newsom Boosted His Book Sales With $1.5 Million From His PAC
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom offered supporters who gave any amount a copy of his book. Roughly 67,000 donors received the memoir, accounting for about two-thirds of its total print sales.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Timothy A. Clary/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Gov. Gavin Newsom of California at an event in February to promote his memoir, “Young Man in a Hurry: A Memoir of Discovery.”

item.title: Despite Cease-Fire, Iran’s Hackers Haven’t Logged Off
item.description: Tehran’s digital warriors have continued to seek ways to gain an advantage in the conflict in a new phase of cyberspace operations.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators in Tehran this week. Iran is trying to keep up pressure on the United States and Israel with cyberattacks but also positioning itself to mount a bigger retaliation if peace talks do not resume.

item.title: Are You an International Student Looking for Work?
item.description: We want to understand how international students are navigating the current job market in the United States.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: We want to understand how international students are navigating the employment market.

item.title: Woman Who Took 15 Tequila Shots on Carnival Cruise Gets $300,000 in Damages
item.description: The woman, who fell and injured herself, said in a lawsuit that bartenders had been negligent for serving her while she was visibly intoxicated.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Allen J. Schaben/Los Angeles Times, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A jury found Carnival responsible for some of the injuries and distress a customer said she had experienced after being served 15 tequila shots.

item.title: House Votes to Preserve Deportation Protections for Haitians, Rebuking Trump
item.description: The action was largely symbolic since the president would be all but certain to veto the bill, but the bipartisan vote reflected resistance within his own ranks on his signature issue.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Ayanna S. Pressley, Democrat of Massachusetts, in January. On Thursday, she accused her Republican colleagues of misrepresenting the Haitian population.

item.title: Panel Advances Trump’s Triumphal Arch, Even as Key Member Suggests Changes
item.description: The Commission of Fine Arts, a Trump-aligned advisory body, granted preliminary approval. But its vice chairman suggested losing statues atop the structure and other revisions before a final vote.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with a model of his arch at the White House in October.

item.title: Hegseth Again Threatens Attacks on Iran’s Civilian Infrastructure
item.description: The U.S. blockade of shipments through the Strait of Hormuz would last “for as long as it takes,” the defense secretary said.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We’d rather not have to do it, but we’re ready to go at the command of our president and at the push of a button,” Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said on Thursday.

item.title: Judge Again Halts Aboveground Construction on Trump Ballroom
item.description: A federal judge wrote that an exception he made for work on security features did not cover most of the construction on the larger ballroom project Trump has proposed.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Cranes over the former East Wing location at the White House earlier this month.

item.title: Venezuelan Doctor Ezequiel Veliz in Texas Is Released From Detention
item.description: Dr. Ezequiel Veliz, a family physician who worked in an area that has a doctor shortage, was detained for 10 days. Another Venezuelan doctor remains locked up.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan and John Yoon
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Ezequiel Veliz, one of the Venezuelan physicians detained by immigration agents in South Texas, has been released.

item.title: ICE Agent Charged With Assault After Motorists Say He Brandished Gun
item.description: Minnesota prosecutors have spent weeks investigating the conduct of immigration agents who took part in an immigration crackdown in the Twin Cities. This is the first case they have brought.
item.dc:creator: Sheila M. Eldred, Ernesto Londoño and Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: David Guttenfelder/The New York Times
item.media:description: Thousands of federal agents were sent to the Twin Cities during a federal crackdown that started late last year.

item.title: House Again Thwarts Challenge to Trump’s Authority to Continue Iran War
item.description: The G.O.P. narrowly blocked a Democratic war powers resolution, but a senior Republican suggested that backing for the conflict is not open-ended and could wane as a statutory deadline approaches in weeks.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison and Megan Mineiro
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We are standing at the edge of a cliff, and Congress must act before this president pushes us off,” Representative Gregory W. Meeks of New York, the top Democrat on the House Foreign Affairs Committee, said ahead of the vote.

item.title: ICE Has Detained Marie-Thérèse Ross-Mahé, an 85-Year-Old Widow
item.description: After Marie-Thérèse Ross-Mahé’s husband died, an inheritance battle exploded. Her stepson then used his influence to have her arrested, an Alabama probate judge said.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Catherine Porter

item.title: Two Magicians Warn the Supreme Court About Junk Science
item.description: Penn & Teller filed a Supreme Court brief questioning the use of “investigative hypnosis” in a death-penalty case in Texas.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Roger Kisby for The New York Times
item.media:description: Penn Jillette and Teller in Las Vegas last year.

item.title: Justice Dept. Appealing Surveillance Court Ruling Amid Congressional Impasse
item.description: The court had barred national security agencies from using certain tools to process Americans’ data gathered under Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, which expires on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The headquarters of the Justice Department in February. The department told Congress it is appealing a court ruling about a surveillance program now under review by lawmakers.

item.title: White House Shrugs Off Shaky Economy as War Exceeds Trump’s Timeline
item.description: Stocks may be soaring again, but the war in Iran has started to pinch the finances of many Americans.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
White House Council of Economic Advisers
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: The energy surge has made groceries more expensive while also raising costs for farmers.

item.title: Chicago Teachers Union, Brandon Johnson Feuding Over School on May Day
item.description: Mayor Brandon Johnson, whose ties to the Chicago Teachers Union helped him win office, has signaled support. The school district and some parents object.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Brandon Johnson of Chicago speaking at a news conference in 2025 with other Illinois officials, including Gov. JB Pritzker.

item.title: Pilots Went Viral for Meowing on the Radio. The F.A.A. Was Not Amused.
item.description: The Federal Aviation Administration said that it was investigating the interaction, and that pilots were banned from “nonessential communication” below a certain altitude.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.media:credit: Kent Nishimura for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Conversations must be related to the safe operation of the aircraft,” the F.A.A. said.

item.title: Virginia Ex-Lt. Gov. Justin Fairfax Kills Wife and Self, Police Say
item.description: Mr. Fairfax served as lieutenant governor from 2018 to 2022. The couple’s teenage children were home, the police said, and one of them called 911.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson, JoAnna Daemmrich and Jacey Fortin
item.media:credit: Parker Michels-Boyce for The New York Times
item.media:description: Justin Fairfax in 2019. He served as Virginia’s lieutenant governor from 2018 to 2022.

item.title: Who’s Winning the Cash Race in 9 Top Senate Contests?
item.description: Money from Democratic donors has flowed into the coffers of the party’s top Senate candidates — especially its nominee in Texas.
item.dc:creator: Matt Zdun and Katherine Chui

item.title: Talarico Leads a Democratic Cash Surge: 7 Takeaways From 2026 Filings
item.description: Democrats in key Senate races out-raised their Republican rivals, but super PACs on the right are poised to play a powerful role in the midterms, new campaign finance filings showed.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico, the Democratic nominee for Senate in Texas, raised $27 million in the first months of 2026, a remarkable haul.

item.title: RFK Jr. Shifts Tone on Vaccines in Congressional Hearing
item.description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who has sought to roll back longstanding vaccine policy, testified that the measles vaccine is safe and effective “for most people” and agreed it was safer than getting measles.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg and Dani Blum
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Democrats assailed Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. over his past statements on health policy.

item.title: The First Statewide Ban on Data Centers Is on the Verge of Becoming Law
item.description: The Maine Legislature approved an 18-month moratorium, but Gov. Janet Mills, a Democrat running for Senate, has yet to say whether she will sign it.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: A data center under construction in Vernon, Calif.

item.title: Jesus Memes, Threats and a War in Iran: A Portrait of Trump Under Pressure
item.description: President Trump has lashed out at enemies, allies and even the pope, and made it harder for Republicans to keep the focus on economic issues in a midterm election year.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at a U.F.C. fight in Miami last week.

item.title: Ahead of 2028, Vance Collects Cash, Chits and Contacts
item.description: The vice president is also the finance chair of the Republican National Committee, allowing him to court donors who could prove helpful should he run for president.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer and Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance has raised over $60 million to help the Republican Party in the midterms, and simultaneously forged relationships with major donors that could help him in his own presidential run.

item.title: Mounting Troubles at Ohio State University Lead to Frustration
item.description: Ohio State isn’t the only university in turmoil, but few others have faced so many issues lately. One lawmaker called the school “a national embarrassment.”
item.dc:creator: Billy Witz
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ohio State University recently named Ravi Bellamkonda, middle, as president. He’s the fourth person to hold the post in six years.

item.title: More Young Men Say Religion Is ‘Very Important’ to Them, Poll Finds
item.description: “I wanted something new and something traditional and something that felt holy.”
item.dc:creator: Ruth Igielnik and Ruth Graham
item.media:credit: Cornell Watson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Worshipers of the All Saints Orthodox Church before a morning service in Raleigh, N.C.
